schools.summary: Ellicott fixes the battle in the plain of Jezreel and reads the collective 'man of Israel'; Keil frames vv.1-14 as the introduction to David's kingship, the Chronicler recording only Saul's death.
Clarke lays out the chapter's outline and points to the near-identical account in 1 Samuel 31; Wesley: princes sin and the people suffer, so great men must beware of provoking God's wrath.
Henry: princes sin and the people suffer, parents sin and children fall with them; not idolatry but the sin of Saul was chiefly in view.
Ellicott: 'clave to Saul' (hotly pursued); the three sons fall while Eshbaal was absent (2 Samuel 2:8); Saul, like Zedekiah, may have watched his sons die — 'in their deaths they were not divided.'
Henry: the sons perish with the guilty father, Jonathan among them; those who love their seed must leave their sins.

themes: the death of Jonathan and his brothers

schools.summary: Ellicott: the battle 'heavy upon' Saul, the archers' arrows and his shudder (a word for writhing/travail); the terror of a king who believed himself forsaken of God; the bow an Egyptian-Philistine arm.
Henry: the archers who hit Saul answer the javelin he had thrown at David — his fall matched his sin point by point.

themes: the terror of a forsaken king

schools.summary: Ellicott: Saul's plea echoes Abimelech's (Judges 9:54); his dread of being 'made a toy of' by the uncircumcised; he falls on 'the sword,' his own.
Henry: he who bade Doeg slay the priests now bids his armour-bearer slay him, and the murderer of the priests is left to be his own murderer.

themes: poetic justice for the priests of Nob

schools.summary: Ellicott: the armour-bearer falls on his own sword and dies, from loyalty to his chief and dread of the foe; several small textual differences from Samuel (brevity of the Chronicler).
Henry: the armour-bearer disobeys Saul's order to kill him, echoing Saul's own disobedience over Amalek.
Clarke: every branch that followed Saul to war was cut off, the three sons chief; Wesley: 'all his house' means the children then present, since Ishbosheth and Mephibosheth were not slain.

Ellicott: it is pedantry to press 'all his house' against Samuel's 'all his men'; the phrase marks the completeness of the overthrow, though the Chronicler knew (1 Chronicles 9:35) some of Saul's house were absent.

themes: the surviving remnant of Saul's house

schools.summary: Ellicott: those 'in the plain' of Jezreel, the people of the surrounding districts, deserted their cities, which the Philistines occupied; a nicety of the masculine pronoun 'in them.'
Henry: the abandoned cities show that princes sin and the people suffer for it.
Wesley: princes sin and the people bear it; great men must beware of kindling God's wrath.

themes: the reach of a king's sin

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'his sons' for Samuel's 'his three sons,' the verse otherwise word for word the same — the Chronicler follows his source closely.
Henry: the greater a man's dignity, the greater his danger of disgrace; Saul's body was abused the more because he was king — advancement makes a man a mark.
Ellicott: they carried off Saul's head (cf. Genesis 40:19) and sent it to bear tidings to their idols, betraying a local conception of gods who could not see beyond their temples (cf. 1 Kings 20:23).
Henry: poor idols, that knew nothing until the news was brought and could do nothing then; the living God who ordered the battle needs no messenger.

Ellicott: the armour set in the house of Ashtaroth (the 'queen of heaven,' Ishtar) and the skull (gulgoleth, cf. Golgotha) fastened in Dagon's temple; the Chronicler supplies the head where Samuel gives the corpse at Beth-shan.
Wesley: if we give not God the glory of our successes, the Philistines who honour Dagon will rise up and condemn us.
Henry: the heathen credit Dagon with the victory; shall the true God be forgotten in our triumphs?
Clarke: the men of Jabesh-gilead acted from heroism and gratitude, remembering Saul's rescue of their city from Nahash (1 Samuel 11); he defers a fuller illustration to the chapter's close.

Ellicott: the night-march and rescue 'from the wall of Beth-shan,' the disputed 'burning' (a degradation, or a burning of spices for a king), the burial of the bones under the terebinth, the seven days' fast — the divergences pointing to another source.
Clarke: the deed belongs to the ancient honour paid to a fallen chief's remains, as Homer sang of the contests over the dead (Patroclus, Hector).

themes: the rescue and burial of Saul

schools.summary: Ellicott: Saul's twofold unfaithfulness — not keeping the prophetic word (1 Samuel 13:13; 15:11) and consulting a necromancer against Leviticus 19:31; the text names no other charge.
Clarke reports the Targum's fuller verdict (Amalek, the diviners, the priests of Nob); Wesley marks the aggravation of sinning against God's express command.
Gill: a violent and dishonourable death for his sins; the Targum's charge about the priests of Nob 'is not in the text.'
Henry: sin finds the sinner out, no greatness exempts, disobedience is a killing thing, and consulting witches fills the measure of iniquity.

debates.question: Does Saul's guilt here include his slaughter of the priests of Nob?

sides.position: The Targum numbers the slaughter of the priests of Nob among the causes of Saul's death
The biblical text makes no allusion to it; only the two named sins are in view

schools.summary: Ellicott traces the failure to Saul's impatience (1 Samuel 13:13), reconciles 'inquired not' with 1 Samuel 28:6, and reads 'he slew him' as God acting through the Philistines and Saul's own hand (as through the Assyrian and Cyrus); Keil affirms the verdict and reads the chapter as the threshold of David's reign.
Gill: Saul's inquiry was external and hypocritical, so accounted as no inquiry at all.
Wesley: Saul did enquire in some sort, but not humbly, diligently, or perseveringly, nor till the last extremity, when it was too late.
Henry: Saul slew himself, yet God slew him; what is done by wicked hands is done by God's determinate counsel, and those who give themselves to the devil are abandoned to him.
